Featured Investigation

Current Investigation  ·  Biodiversity & Pandemic Science

What the Village Chicken Knows

From a dusty Kenyan homestead to the frontier of pandemic science, the indigenous village chicken reveals hidden lessons about resilience, evolution, immunity, and genomic adaptation.

Enter Exhibit
I

Gallery I

The Helix Root

The Origin Room

The Helix Root is an act of reclaiming. By mastering wet-to-dry lab transitions and building localized infrastructure literacy, we are asserting that those who possess the roots — the indigenous genetic history of our people — must hold the keys to the future of medicine.

"Before the first laboratory was built. Before the first genome was sequenced. Before science had the language to describe what it was looking at — the story was already written in every cell of every person who called this continent home."

— The Genomic Continent, Sequence Africa, May 2026

Founding Essay

Africa Is Sequencing — The Mission

Four pillars. One continent. A molecular biologist writing from inside the work — not to explain Africa to the world, but to document Africa's science for Africa itself.

Read the founding essay

Exhibit 1.1

The Genomic Continent: Africa's DNA Has Always Known

The San. The Yoruba. The Hadza. Lineages carrying genetic signatures that predate every other story told by global genomics.

Ancient DNAOrigins Read

Exhibit 1.2

The Archive Is Open. Now How Do We Protect It?

A fortress or a village square? The Ubuntu Network model: not one vault, but many connected hubs. The intelligence is shared. The ownership is not.

Data SovereigntyUbuntu Network Read

A Living Archive

Timeline of African Genomics

2003
Human Genome Project Completed
The first complete human genome sequence is published. African populations contributed yet remain dramatically underrepresented in subsequent research databases.
2013
H3Africa Launched
The Human Heredity and Health in Africa initiative launches — a landmark consortium building genomics infrastructure, training researchers, and producing science that serves African populations first.
2020
African COVID Sequencing Response
African scientists sequence and track SARS-CoV-2 variants in real time, demonstrating the continent's growing genomic surveillance capacity and the critical importance of African-led science.
2025
African BioGenome Project Expansion
A continental mission to sequence all of Africa's plant, animal, and fungal species accelerates. The ambition: the most comprehensive biological inventory of any continent on earth.
Future
One Million African Genomes
The horizon Sequence Africa is documenting in real time: a future where African genetic data is owned, controlled, and interpreted by Africans — for the benefit of African health and sovereignty.

Exhibit 2.1 · Indigenous Livestock

What the Village Chicken Knows

Indigenous East African chickens may carry pandemic resistance to H5N1 that commercial breeds never developed. The tools to characterize this exist. The question is who leads the research.

H5N1BiodiversityPandemic Science Read

Exhibit 2.2 · Botanical Heritage

Roots Without Receipts

The Prunus africana tree from Kenya's highlands underpins a $150M global market in prostate medication. The communities whose ancestral knowledge identified this plant have no benefit-sharing agreement.

BiopiracyNagoya Protocol Read
III

Gallery III

The Engine of Discovery

The Laboratory Wing

We do not just display breakthroughs; we demystify the process. We highlight the operational reality of sequencing facilities across Africa, the development of robust bioinformatics pipelines, and the necessity of localized validation frameworks for oncology.

"The most dangerous failure in a bioinformatics pipeline doesn't crash with an error message. It runs to completion cleanly while producing results calibrated to a population that isn't yours."

— A Training Scenario for African Biosovereignty, Sequence Africa, June 2026

Exhibit 3.1 · Pipeline Bias

A Training Scenario for African Biosovereignty

An East African TNBC researcher validates NGS results against databases where East African populations are effectively invisible. A scenario-based training framework for catching what the pipeline cannot.

OncologyTNBCNGS Read the case study

Exhibit 3.2 · Training Design

Beyond the Syllabus

The wet-to-dry gap is real. Researchers who can run samples cannot always interpret the output. This exhibit documents a pedagogical framework to bridge it, built for the African context.

EducationWet-to-Dry Read

Exhibit 3.3 · Skills Infrastructure

Building the Factories. Not the Scientists.

"We do not need more manufacturing capacity if we have not developed the skills to operate what we already have." A field dispatch from the University of Nairobi.

Field ReportManufacturing Read
IV

Gallery IV

Facing the Unflinching Truth

The Extraction Archive

This is an honest, unflinching record of the processes that have historically facilitated the movement of Africa's biological resources beyond our borders. By archiving what was taken, we articulate what must be protected.

"The Prunus africana tree has been harvested from Kenya's highland forests for decades. A $150 million global market in prostate medication exists because of knowledge passed down through generations of indigenous communities. There is no benefit-sharing agreement."

— Roots Without Receipts, Sequence Africa, June 2026

Case File 4.1 · Biopiracy

The Prunus Africana Case — Knowledge Without Compensation

A documented account of how traditional knowledge from Kenya's Kikuyu, Meru, and Kamba communities was incorporated into a globally profitable pharmaceutical product without acknowledgment, compensation, or benefit-sharing. The Nagoya Protocol exists to prevent this. Its implementation remains contested.

Prunus AfricanaNagoya ProtocolKenyaBiopiracy Read the full case study

Case File 4.2 · Coming Soon

Colonial Plant Collections and the Natural History Archives

The botanical specimens collected across East and Central Africa between 1880 and 1960, now held in overseas archives. What repatriation would mean for African science.

In research

Case File 4.3 · Coming Soon

Genomic Data Extraction — Whose Cloud Is It On?

African genomic data routinely stored on servers in Europe and North America. The legal frameworks, the gaps, and the advocates fighting for on-continent data residency.

In research

Enter Exhibit

The Sovereign Scientist

Portraits of African researchers reclaiming their continent's biology

Science has a face. In Africa, that face is rarely the one on the cover of global journals; it is the face of a researcher reclaiming the continent's biology on their own terms. The Sovereign Scientist chronicles twenty-five African researchers: the Sequence, Plant, Animal, and Human Keepers, and the Builders who are shifting the narrative from beneficiaries of research to primary agents of a sovereign scientific project. This collection is not merely a portrait of individuals; it is a portrait of Africa's biological future. We are currently in the process of curating these twenty-five stories. This Reading Room will soon hold the physical declaration of our continent's inheritance.

Twenty-Five Scientists

Public Education

Science for Everyone

The Education Hub

African genomics should not be a closed conversation. This hub opens the science to anyone who is curious — from secondary school students to policymakers to the families whose DNA holds the answers.

What is DNA?

Deoxyribonucleic acid — the molecule inside every living cell that carries the instructions for life. If your body is a library, DNA is every book inside it.

Coming soon — full exhibit
What is a Genome?

The complete set of all DNA in an organism. Your genome contains roughly 3 billion base pairs — and Africa holds more genomic diversity than all other continents combined.

Coming soon — full exhibit
What is Sequencing?

The process of reading the order of DNA bases — like transcribing a book letter by letter, except the book has 3 billion characters written in only four letters: A, T, C, G.

Coming soon — full exhibit
What is NGS?

Next-Generation Sequencing — a technology that sequences millions of DNA fragments simultaneously. It transformed genomics from a decade-long project into a process that takes hours.

Coming soon — full exhibit
What is Bioinformatics?

The science of making sense of biological data using computers. When you sequence a genome, you get billions of data points. Bioinformatics is how you read the story inside them.

Coming soon — full exhibit
What is CRISPR?

A molecular tool that allows scientists to edit DNA with precision. Derived from a bacterial immune system, it is now being explored for treating sickle cell disease — highly prevalent in Africa.

Coming soon — full exhibit
VII

Gallery VII

The Human Archive

The Voices Chamber

African scientists, researchers, ethicists, and policymakers speaking in their own words — through interviews, essays, and recorded conversations. The science is not separate from the people who do it.

"We do not need more manufacturing capacity if we have not developed the skills to operate what we already have."

— PS Dr. Ouma Oluga · Principal Secretary, Ministry of Health Kenya · University of Nairobi Forum, June 2026

"The question is not whether Africa can sequence its genome. The question is whether Africans will control what that sequencing means."

— Joy Manyasi Kabaka · Sequence Africa · Founding Essay, May 2026

"Having the data is only the beginning. A fortress or a village square? We need the intelligence to be shared but the ownership to be ours."

— Sequence Africa · The Archive Is Open, May 2026
